channel.title: Comments for page "They Who Find Darkness Loving"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "They Who Find Darkness Loving"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this tale! I actually want to know more about the character and what exactly happened. I also like the whole antimemetics aspect of it. I hope to see more of the same soon!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not arguing with most of this (though I obviously disagree), but:

I found the constant inclusion of hyperlinked material extremely jarring and borderline insulting to the reader.

Only if the reader is assumed to be a Foundation veteran and no one else.

I am a very strong supporter of this style of crosslink because they're the kind of shit that would make me incredibly happy as a new reader. And I don't have any issue with them as a vet.

It's not REMEMBER THIS? It's "Hey here's another cool thing related to this. Here is more fun for you to have if you like this."

If you find it jarring, alright, but it's not an insult.

The 'crutch' accusation has no basis here. It's being applied outside its correct context. There's also no such thing as 'undeserved' crosslink. There's no literary or creative basis for 'earning' a crosslink unless the material itself is actually content-free without it.

In this case, the tale in no way relies on any of the crosslinks and would stand exactly the same without it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found myself disliking this to an oddly powerful degree, something which rarely occurs with me as long as the text is competently written, which this is. I believe my problem with it can be divided into two:

  • Scatter: I found the connecting thread between the different scenes of the tale to be particularly weak, something which was further emphasized by the use of a different SCP/character in near every section. I didn't find Solace nearly a compelling enough character to form a bedrock for the tale, as they seem to mostly walk throughout their day as if dazed and barely aware of where they are. This is perhaps intentional, but it nevertheless made for a somewhat irritating read. No scene is concluded satisfactorily, instead they all just sorta fade away at a seemingly arbitrary point.
  • Bluntness: I found the constant inclusion of hyperlinked material extremely jarring and borderline insulting to the reader. Cross references are fine if done with a reasonable degree of subtlety, but here they are presented with all the finesse of a brick to the head. The hyperlinks seem to scream to the reader "REMEMBER THIS? YOU READ THIS". If the reader cannot be expected to recognize the characters/SCPs without such bluntness, I think their inclusion, at least in this manner should perhaps be reconsidered. In short, the text simply does not have enough in it to merit this amount of crosslinks. They feel undeserved and shoddy, used as a crutch instead of as something to add to an already fully realized text.

Due to these reasons, I feel I must downvote. The character of Solace, while not really fitting to my own perception of the Foundation, is not without merit, but as it is presented here it is simply not complete enough to carry a story on its own, in my opinion at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. Lament is an ink monster because Troy is a pretty shadowy fella.
2. The researcher is a they because they're neither a she nor a he.
3. Because it takes place in SCP-universe and involves a lot of stuff?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I couldn't really get into this. When did Lament become an ink monster? Why is this researcher a "they"? Why all the freaking crosslinks? Too weird. (Possibly the strangest thing I've ever said about anything here.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would be a waste of a character to not use this version of Lament again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought this was fantastic.

The hidden text adds a lot, though I'm not totally sure it should be hidden; I wish I'd read this once through without the hidden text so I'd be able to give a better opinion on that.

Solace is a very compelling character. I find this almost surprising, given that this stemmed from a silly joke, and is partially based on an anomalous character trait a million and one newbies have tried (and failed) to pull off.

It partly works because of the really nice writing (a lot of good lines in here). It also works because you play a lot of it fairly straight-faced; if you'd tried to go goofier, it wouldn't have worked.

I enjoyed the usage of previously established characters as well (and not just the expected ones). Made this character and story feel like they fit into a larger universe.

item.title: I still have no idea what to do with that troy-senpai. :<
(no title)
item.content:encoded: We liked this. The hidden text comes off as a bit fiddly and inconsistent, but the portrayal of Agent Lament is a promising one. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found Solace to be a thoroughly boring character, the references to other self-insert characters to be clunky, out-of-place, and self-indulgent, and the core principle did not strike me as particularly gripping.

As a result the attempts at any sort of emotional impact were dead on arrival.

Downvoted for all of the above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm so jealous! I was going to write a story about someone immune to anomalies. *sigh*
That aside, the story is fan-freaking-tastic! Please, please, please make this into a series! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A tale about Dr. Solace the neurodivergent, agendered, mind-anomaly immune doctor and their maybe-halluctination Ichor!Lament. If this is well-received I might make this into a series.

Don't forget to pay attention.
